rationale_for_baseline_tier: Near-syncope, tachycardia and heavy ongoing fluid loss in a patient living alone indicate significant volume depletion needing IV rehydration and an in-person abdominal assessment, and the food-triggered flushing/GI/near-syncope combination means anaphylaxis must be considered. None of this can be examined or treated via telehealth, so urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment (T4) is proportionate. T3 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 (adrenaline + 000) for airway/breathing/collapse, or to ED for a rigid abdomen or GI bleeding.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Throat tightness/voice change/wheeze, true collapse, or worsening faintness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Use adrenaline if available and call 000 immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates anaphylaxis or circulatory collapse requiring emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your throat tightens, you wheeze, you collapse, or the faintness worsens — use your adrenaline pen if you have one and call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rigid board-like abdomen, blood in the stool, or inability to pass urine
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests a surgical abdomen or severe complication.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your stomach becomes hard like a board, you pass blood, or you can't pass urine, go to ED or call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to attend safely or keep any fluids down
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call an ambulance for same-day assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: Dehydration with isolation may require ambulance transport for assessment/IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't safely get to hospital or can't keep fluids down, call an ambulance to be assessed today.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with heavy fluid loss and near-syncope
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises urgency and the case for ambulance transport; arrange support and monitoring.
Flags anaphylaxis risk and the need for an adrenaline plan and specialist review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your gut has reacted severely to a food and you're losing a lot of fluid, which is why you feel faint and your heart is racing — you need to be seen in person today, most likely for a drip and a proper tummy check. Sip rehydration fluids, sit or lie down to avoid fainting, and arrange to get to hospital.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend ED / urgent same-day assessment today (ambulance if you can't get there safely); use adrenaline + 000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use ED/000 — this needs same-day assessment regardless of the hour.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the food-triggered GI flare with flushing, near-syncope and tachycardia, heavy fluid loss, and that IV fluids and an abdominal/anaphylaxis assessment are needed.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Advising home management for a near-syncopal, tachycardic, dehydrated patient with a food-triggered reaction, missing anaphylaxis, a surgical abdomen or collapse.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient collapses at home from dehydration or an unrecognised anaphylactic component while alone.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Immediate ambulance/000 when urgent same-day ED self-presentation (T4) would suffice for a currently stable-but-dehydrated patient — acceptable but resource-heavy.
